segments.s209-g121: This noun phrase literally reads 'many head caught (in) trap (of) lasso.' بس(ی) (here with meaning 'many' in poetic use) precedes the nominal clause; 'سر گرفتار' is noun + postpositive adjective 'caught' (گرفتار). 'دام کمند' is a genitive-like apposition where دام (trap) is specified by کمند (lasso). The finite verb is omitted (typical in poetry) and the phrase is understood as 'many heads are caught in the lasso's trap.'
segments.s209-g122: Again 'بسی' (poetic 'many') modifies the whole clause. 'خوار گشته' is a verb/adjectival phrase meaning 'has become despised/low' with گشته acting as a resultative 'become' and خوار the predicate adjective. 'تن ارجمند' is the noun 'body' + postposed adjective 'esteemed' (literally 'esteemed body') which functions as the subject that 'has become' خوار; the copula is omitted.
segments.s209-g123: A series of nouns linked by و: 'کفن جوشن و بستر' (shroud, cuirass, and bedding). The prepositional phrase 'از خون و خاک' ('from/of blood and dust') modifies these items, indicating they are stained with or made of blood and dust. Word order is noun(s) + modifying prepositional phrase.
segments.s209-g124: 'تن نازدیده' is 'the body not used to fondness/tenderness' (نازدیده here = unused to being caressed). The instrumental prefix بـ- attaches to 'شمشیر' producing 'بشمشیر' = 'by/with the sword', and 'چاک' is a resultant adjective/verb meaning 'torn/split.' The clause thus reads literally 'the body, unaccustomed to fondness, (is) split by the sword' with the copula omitted.
segments.s209-g125: 'زمین ارغوان' = 'earth (is) crimson' and 'زمان سندروس' = 'time/age (is) sandalwood/scented with sandalwood.' Adjectives follow their nouns (or are apposed) and the copula 'is' is omitted in the poetic line, so each noun is being described by the following modifier.
segments.s209-g126: 'سپهر و ستاره' = 'the sky and the stars.' 'پرآوای کوس' is a noun phrase where 'پرآوای' ('full-voiced' / 'resounding') modifies 'کوس' (war-drum), so the image is that the heavens and stars are like a loudly sounding drum. Again the copula is omitted; the modifier precedes the head noun in this compound.
segments.s209-g127: Conditional 'اگر' introduces the clause. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'تاج جوید' (verb + object: 'seeks the crown') is placed before its subject 'جهانجوی مرد' ('the man who seeks the world/the ambitious man'). 'جوید' is the present/subjunctive form 'seeks/longs for.'
segments.s209-g128: 'وگر' ('but/and if') introduces the alternative conditional. 'خاک گردد' = 'becomes dust' with گردد as 'become' (subjunctive/optative in conditional use). 'بروز نبرد' is a temporal genitive-like phrase 'on the day of battle' ('بروز' = 'day/occasion' modifying 'نبرد' = 'battle'). The clause lacks an overt main verb for poetic conciseness, the meaning being 'or if he becomes dust on the day of battle.'
segments.s209-g169: همه یکسره گرزها برکشیم is an exhortative first‑person plural clause. همه یکسره = "all at once / altogether" (همه = all; یکسره here = straightaway/at once). گرزها is the plural definite/indefinite noun "maces/mauls" and appears before the verb. برکشیم is the present‑subjunctive/optative first‑person plural of برکشیدن (prefix بر- + کشیدن) meaning "let us lift/raise"; the verb form functions like an English "let us ...".
segments.s209-g170: یکی از لب رود برتر کشیم is a continuation: یکی = "one" (one of them). از لبِ رود = a prepositional + genitive phrase "from/of the river's lip/brink" (از marking origin, لبِ رود = riverbank). برتر here functions adverbially/qualificationally = "higher/more above" modifying کشیم. کشیم is again first‑person plural subjunctive of کشیدن (used here as "let us raise/draw up" in context); the literal sense is "let us raise one above the riverbank."
segments.s209-g171: بانبوه رزمی بسازیم سخت: بانبوه (با + نُبوه) = "in great numbers / abundantly" and functions adverbially. رزمی (from رزم) is an adjective/noun meaning "martial / of battle" (i.e. a fighting force). بسازیم is first‑person plural subjunctive/optative of ساختن/ساختن used as "let us make/let us form." سخت follows and qualifies the action or the resulting force as "hard/strong/ferocious" (here conveying "a strong/terrible fighting host").
segments.s209-g172: اگر یار باشد جهاندار و بخت is a conditional clause: اگر = "if." یار = "ally/companion" (here meaning an ally on our side). باشد is third‑person singular present/subjunctive of بودن used in the protasis ("if there be / if there is"). جهاندار و بخت are the two items presented as possible allies — "the sovereign (world‑ruler) and Fate" — i.e. "if the ruler and Fate are (our) allies." The syntax is elliptical and typical of epic Persian: the copula は/باشد links یار to the following appositive nouns.
segments.s209-g145: چو here is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when'. گفتار (literally 'speech' or 'words') is the noun subject of the clause; پور ('son, youth') specifies whose speech it is. زره شد is a past event: شد 'became' with زره 'armor' as its complement (classical Persian often uses 'شد' to mean 'was donned' or 'became armored'). ببن is an interjection/imperative 'behold/see' appended for emphasis. The clause reads as a temporal clause: 'When the young man’s words were spoken and he was armed — behold!'
segments.s209-g146: This is a straightforward SOV sentence: سپهدار ایران 'the commander of Iran' is the subject (title + genitive), شنید is the past-tense verb 'heard', and این سخن 'these/this words' is the object. The past simple شنید indicates a completed action (he heard what was said).
segments.s209-g147: بپاسخ functions as the noun 'reply/answer' (object), followed by ندید 'he did not see' (past negative sense). ایچ modifies رای and means 'no/none at all'; رای درنگ is a bound noun phrase 'counsel/plan of delay' (رای = counsel/plan, درنگ = delay). The whole segment means 'He perceived no answer, no counsel for delaying.'
segments.s209-g148: همان is emphatic 'the very/same'. آبداری here denotes a water-container or something that serves/provides water (a water-vessel or water-bearer). The relative clause که بودش بچنگ modifies آبداری: که 'which', بودش = بود + ش 'was (his/it was his)' (بود in past with enclitic pronoun), and بچنگ 'in (the) grasp/in his hand'. So: 'the very water-vessel that was in his hand.'
segments.s209-g149: بزد is a past tense verb 'he struck/struck down'. بر سر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the head'. The conjunction و links another noun phrase ترگ آن نامدار 'the rag/shred of that renowned one' (ترگ = rag/shred, آن نامدار = that famed one). The verb precedes the coordinated prepositional/noun phrases, a common poetic word order.
segments.s209-g150: تو is the vocative/pronoun 'you'. گفتی here functions as the past-tense 'you said' in context (note: the provided sense list does not include the verbal past-tense sense, so see senses mapping). تنش is تن 'body' plus the enclitic ش 'his' = 'his body'. سر نیاورد بار is an expression: سر (head) is the object, نیاورد is the past negative 'did not bring/carry', and بار 'burden' clarifies the sense of carrying. The clause conveys 'you said his body would not / could not carry his head as a burden' (i.e., his head was taken or not borne by the body).
segments.s209-g151: برآمد is a past/punctiliar verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز ایران is the source 'from Iran'. سپه is 'the army/host' (subject), and بوق و کوس are the instruments/attributes 'horns and drums' (the phrase lists the martial signals that went up with the host). The order places the verb first for poetic force and then gives origin and instruments.
segments.s209-g152: که introduces a wish/explanatory clause ('that' / 'may it be that'). پیروز is an adjective 'victorious'; بادا is an optative particle 'may it be'. سرافراز is another adjective 'triumphant/exalted', and طوس is the proper name Tūs (the hero). The sequence is a wish: 'may Tūs be victorious and exalted.'
segments.s209-g57: بر (preposition) here means 'against' and takes the following noun phrase شاه ایران ('the Shah of Iran') as its object. شوی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive form of شدن used here in an intransitive sense 'you go/set yourself' (poetic/instructive tone), and با سپاه ('with an army') is an instrumental/adverbial phrase placed after the verb.
segments.s209-g58: مکافات is the direct object 'recompense/reward'. یابی (2nd person) means 'you will find/obtain' (present/future sense). به نیکی is an adverbial phrase 'with kindness / kindly' (به used adverbially), and ز شاه is a poetic 'from the Shah' showing the source of the recompense.
segments.s209-g59: بایران here is a poetic contraction of به ایران meaning 'in/for Iran' (prepositional phrase placed first for emphasis). ترا is the accusative/enclitic form 'to you' (ت + را), پهلوانی is an abstract noun 'heroic status / heroism', and دهد is 3rd-person singular 'gives / will give' — so the clause says 'he gives you heroism in Iran' (i.e., grants you hero-status).
segments.s209-g60: همان 'that same' points back to a specific reward; افسر is 'helmet' and خسروانی is an adjectival qualifier 'Khosrowian / royal (of the king)'. دهد repeats the verb 'gives' (confirming the reward). The noun + adjective order (افسر خسروانی) is noun followed by a postpositive qualifier.
segments.s209-g61: چو is the subordinating particle 'when'. یاد آیدش is a unit meaning 'the memory/remembrance comes to him' (آید + enclitic ش = 'to him'). خوب کردار تو is a noun phrase 'your good deed(s)' with the adjective خوب preceding the noun کردار and the possessor تو after it.
segments.s209-g62: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش). رنجه گردد is a light-verb construction: رنجه (afflicted/distressed) + گردد (becomes) = 'becomes distressed / is grieved'. ز تیمار تو is a causal phrase 'because of / from concern for you' (ز = from/because of, تیمار = solicitous care, تو = your/you).
segments.s209-g63: چنین 'thus' modifies the verb گفت 'said'. The verb precedes its subject (a common epic inversion): after گفت comes the list of speakers—گودرز و گیو ('Goudarz and Giv')—followed by سران 'the chiefs/leaders'.
segments.s209-g64: بزرگان 'the great ones / great men' and تیمارکش 'those who bear care / caretakers' form a coordinated apposition to سران; مهتران here amplifies their senior/leading status ('elders/chiefs'). Word order groups several epithets for the same class of leaders.
segments.s209-g113: ز بس introduces a causal/partitive phrase: ز = “from” (poetic) and بس here functions as a quantifier meaning roughly “so many/so much.” ترگ زرین and زرین سپر are noun + adjective (Persian normally places the adjective after the noun), joined by و “and.” There is no verb in this segment; it is a nominal prepositional phrase that will be paralleled by the next segment.
segments.s209-g114: Another prepositional noun phrase introduced by ز “from.” جوشن سواران is a genitive/possessive grouping (“the cuirasses of the horsemen”); زرین کمر is a noun + adjective meaning “golden girdles/belts.” The two nominal phrases are coordinated by the line rhythm rather than an explicit conjunction.
segments.s209-g115: برآمد is a past tense verb “arose/issued forth,” placed before the nominal phrase (verb–subject inversion typical in poetry). یکی ابر = “a certain/a single cloud.” چون introduces a simile: چون سندروس = “like sandalwood/fragrant wood.”
segments.s209-g116: زمین گشت uses گشت (past of گشتن) meaning “became/turned.” از گرد is a causal/agentive phrase meaning “from the dust” (گرد here = dust). چون + آبنوس is the comparative complement: “became like ebony.” The order is subject–verb then the clause indicating cause and the simile.
segments.s209-g117: سر سروران is a genitive construct “the head(s) of the chiefs” (noun + possessor). زیر گرز گران is a locative/condition phrase “under heavy maces” (زیر = under; گرز گران = ‘heavy mace’ with adjective after noun). The whole is a noun phrase setting the scene for the verb in the next segment.
segments.s209-g118: چو is the comparative ‘as/like’; سندان شد = “became like an anvil” with شد (past of شدن) as the copular/transformative verb. و connects the parallel image پتک آهنگران = “the smiths’ hammer(s)” (noun + possessor). The verb شد governs the simile applied to both images.
segments.s209-g119: ز خون رود = “from rivers/streams of blood” (ز introducing the source). گفتی here is a parenthetical/verbalizer meaning roughly “one might say / so it is said” (an idiomatic poetic insertion), and میستان شدست = “became miestān” where میستان in this context is best read as ‘wine-house / tavern’ (a metaphorical image). شدست is an archaic/poetic past form meaning “it became.”
segments.s209-g120: ز نیزه هوا = “from the spears the air” (source + subject). چون نیستان شدست is a simile + verb: چون = “like/as,” نیستان = “reed-bed,” and شدست = “became.” Grammatically the phrase says the air, on account of the spears, became like a reed-bed (i.e., filled with reed-like spears).
segments.s209-g137: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. از دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar / at a distance'. طوس سپهبد is the subject 'Tūs, the spahbed' (title follows the name). بدید is the archaic/past form of دیدن 'to see' placed at the end as the finite verb (classical Persian word order: subordinate phrase + subject + verb).
segments.s209-g138: Two coordinated actions: بغرید is a past verb 'he roared' (perfective), followed by coordinating و 'and'. تیغ از میان برکشید is a verbal phrase 'drew a sword out of the middle/meddle' — از میان 'out of/from the middle' and برکشید (archaic past of برکشیدن) 'drew/raised' the weapon; the implied subject is the same (Tūs).
segments.s209-g139: بپور here is the fused/prepositional phrase 'به پور' meaning 'to Pour…' (addressing Pour-Zereh). زره is the personal name element attached (here part of the addressee's name). گفت is the past 'said' (the reporter verb), followed by the direct question نام تو چیست where نام 'name' + تو 'you' + چی + ست forms the interrogative 'what is your name?'; ست is the classical copula (replacing modern است) and appears in the question form چیست.
segments.s209-g140: ز is the poetic/prepositional form of از 'from/of' marking affiliation 'of the Turks'; ترکان 'the Turks' is the genitive. جنگی here means 'warlike / warrior' (a fighter of the Turks). ترا is the object/indirect-object formed from ت (2nd-person clitic) + را (object marker) meaning 'to you' or 'for you'. یار کیست literally 'who is (a) companion?' — کی 'who' + ست (classical copula) — yielding 'Who is your ally/companion?'.
segments.s209-g141: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = به + او, 'to him'; گفت = 'said' in the past). The reported speech follows: ارژنگ is the speaker's name, جنگی 'a warrior' (predicate/noun), and منم is the emphatic copula 'I am' (من + ـم), asserting identity.
segments.s209-g142: Two coordinated predicates describing the speaker: سرافراز 'honored/triumphant' linked with و 'and'. شیر is used metaphorically 'lion' (brave/valiant). درنگی here is a descriptive/epithet attached to شیر; its precise sense in this line is uncertain in the provided sense list (likely a nisba/epithet or place‑name derivative), and منم repeats the emphatic 'I am'.
segments.s209-g143: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. خاک را is the definite object 'the earth/ground' (را marks it). از تو 'because of/from you' gives the cause/source. رخشان 'radiant' is a predicative adjective and کنم (classical present/subjunctive of کردن) at the end expresses the speaker's intended action—'I will/may make the earth radiant on your account.'
segments.s209-g144: This clause continues the action: به آوردگه is the goal 'to the place/assembly' (به = to/into, آوردگه = venue/place of bringing). برسرافشان is a compound verb-phrase (بر + سرافشان) meaning to scatter/throw upon (literally 'cast upon the head' in the lexical senses) — here idiomatically 'to cast/display/scatter (glory/dust) before the assembly' — and کنم is again the first-person verb 'I will do/make'.
segments.s209-g105: بیاراست is an archaic/poetic past/perfect verb meaning “arrayed/arranged” (verb first for poetic effect). لشکر سپهدار طوس is a genitive/nominal phrase “the army (لشکر) of the commander (سپهدار) Tus (طوس)” with the head noun لشکر following the verb; there is no overt possessive particle — the two nouns form a single attributive phrase.
segments.s209-g106: This clause has a transitive verb کشیدند (past, 3rd plural) taking بهامون “toward us/for us” as a directional/ethical dative; the direct objects پیلان و کوس (elephants and war‑drums) follow. کشیدند here means “drew up/brought (forward).”
segments.s209-g107: دو رویه is a numeral + noun phrase meaning “two‑row / two‑rank / two‑lined” (here rendered as ‘in two ranks’); سپاه is the subject and اندر آمد is the archaic compound verb meaning “came on / advanced” (اندر + آمد). چو introduces a simile (“like”), linking سپاه to کوه (“mountain”) for vivid comparison.
segments.s209-g108: This is a noun list in apposition to the preceding image: سواران (“horsemen”) followed by an identification of their peoples ترکان و ایران (Turks and Iranians), with گروه functioning as a collective noun ‘band / troop.’ Word order is noun(s) then their descriptors.
segments.s209-g109: چنان شد is a fixed poetic sequence meaning “it became so” or “so it was”; ز گرد سپاه literally ‘from the circle/round of the host’ uses ز (poetic 'from/because of') plus گرد as ‘round/circumference’ to describe the host’s radiance, and آفتاب (sun) is the simile or result‑noun — the host is likened to the sun.
segments.s209-g110: که introduces a result or explanatory clause (“so that / such that”); آتش برآمد is a verbal phrase ‘fire arose/issued forth’ (برآمد = arose/emerged), and ز دریای آب uses ز + دریای آب (“from the sea of water”) giving a striking, paradoxical image following the prior brightness.
segments.s209-g111: درخشیدن is an infinitival/masculine noun form used as a substantive here: ‘the shining/glittering of…’. It forms a list with تیغ (swords), ژوپین (gambeson/padded jack) and خشت (here a plate‑like item — poetically part of the armour imagery). The conjunctions و simply join the items in the catalogue.
segments.s209-g112: تو گفتی is a folkloric/poetic formula meaning roughly “one would say / you would say” (idiomatic; the pronoun is generic). شب اندر هوا is a locative phrase ‘night in the air’ and لاله کشت uses کشت in the sense of ‘planting / sowing’ here, producing the metaphor ‘you would say the night had sown tulips in the air.’
segments.s209-g17: پس is a sentence-initial temporal adverb 'then'. لشکر is the subject noun 'army/host'. اندر is the archaic/pre‑poetic preposition 'in/within' (here functioning like 'under the command of'). سپهدار طوس is an appositive noun phrase (سپهدار = the commander/title, طوس = the personal name Tous) that specifies whose army it is. The whole segment forms the subject/agent phrase that precedes the verb.
segments.s209-g18: بیامد is a past simple intransitive verb 'came'. بر + شه marks motion toward/appearance before the king ('بر' = to/toward, شه = king). زمین داد بوس is a light verb construction: زمین 'the ground' (object), داد 'gave' (past of دادن used as light verb), and بوس is the noun 'a kiss' — together 'gave a kiss to the ground', i.e. 'kissed the ground'. The clause order (verb following the subject phrase) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s209-g19: برو here functions as a short exclamatory particle introducing an acclamation; آفرین is the acclamation noun 'bravo, applause' and کرد is the past 'did/made' turning the noun into a performed action ('they cried "Bravo!"'). و is the coordinating 'and'. بر شد (بر + شد) is a compound verbal idea 'rose up/was raised' and خروش is the noun 'roar/uproar' that undergoes that action — together: 'and a roar arose.' Note the noun+کرد light-verb pattern and the use of بر شد to mean 'arose'.
segments.s209-g20: جهان آمد is literal 'the world came' but here means 'the world was stirred/was affected'. از introduces a causal phrase 'from/by', بانگ اسپان is 'the cry/sound of the horses' (بانگ = sound/cry, اسپان = horses). بجوش is a poetic verbal/adjectival element meaning 'to boil/seethe' (used predicatively); so the whole clause says the world was seething because of the horses' cries. Note the causal از phrase placed before the final predicate بجوش, a poetic inversion.
segments.s209-g21: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain' marking a single instance. ابر is the noun 'cloud'. بست is the past form of بستن here meaning 'closed in' or 'gathered/encircled'. از بر گرد سم is a locative/partitive phrase: از 'from', بر + گرد 'around' (گرد = around), سم 'hoof' — together 'from around the hoof' (i.e. a cloud of dust gathered around the hoof). Word order places the verb بست after the subject and before the locative phrase.
segments.s209-g22: برآمد is a past verbal compound meaning 'arose/issued forth'. خروشیدن is a deverbal noun/infinitive 'the roaring' (literally 'to roar') used as the content that arises. گاو دم is a genitive-like juxtaposition: گاو 'ox/cattle' + دم 'breath' (here used for the bellow/lowing); together 'the ox's bellow'. The verb precedes the verbal-noun phrase (typical poetic inversion): 'the ox's bellow arose.'
segments.s209-g23: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' introducing the cause. بس here works as an intensifier 'so many'/'so much' (literally 'many/enough'). جوشن 'cuirasses/armour' and کاویانی درفش 'Kayanian banner' form a coordinated noun phrase (کاویانی modifies درفش: 'Kayanian banners'). The whole segment gives the cause: 'because of so many cuirasses and Kayanian banners.'
segments.s209-g24: شده is a past participle/predicate meaning 'has been made' or 'became' (passive/result). روی گیتی 'the face/surface of the world' (روی = face/surface, گیتی = world). سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout/entire', and بنفش is the color adjective 'purple'. Together they form the result clause: 'the face of the world had become entirely purple.' Note the passive/result state (شده) linking the cause (previous segment) to this outcome.
segments.s209-g65: سراینده is the agent noun 'the poet' (here the speaker/performer). پاسخ آمد is a light-verb expression 'a reply came' = 'replied' (پاسخ as the thing and آمد as the verbalizing element). چو باد is a simile modifying آمد: چو = 'like' and باد = 'the wind', so the whole segment means the reply arrived swiftly 'like the wind'.
segments.s209-g66: بنزدیک is an adverb/prepositional locative 'near' or 'close by'. پیران ویسه نژاد is a noun group 'the elders of Viseh's lineage' — پیران (elders) with the following compound ویسه نژاد indicating the familial/lineage affiliation. The whole segment gives the location/recipients: 'near the elders of Viseh's line.'
segments.s209-g67: بگفت is simple past 'he said' or 'he told'. آنچ بشنید is a fused relative clause 'that which he had heard' (آنچ = 'that which', بشنید = past 'he heard'/'he had heard'). با پهلوان here marks the addressee or accompaniment — 'to/with the champion' (i.e. he related it in the presence of or to the hero).
segments.s209-g68: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or here functioning like 'to/with' in enumerating recipients; طوس is the hero Tūs (not the city) and و connects items. The second ز repeats the preposition for emphasis before گودرز روشن‌روان ('Goudarz, bright‑souled'), so the phrase lists the persons who heard/received the report: Tūs and Goudarz (described as روشن‌روان).
segments.s209-g69: چنین داد پاسخ is a formulaic clause 'thus gave an answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). The conjunction/particle که introduces reported speech or content: 'that ...'. من روز و شب places the speaker ('I') and a time frame 'day and night' as the subject/time adverbial of the forthcoming verb.
segments.s209-g70: بیاد سپهبد is an adverbial phrase 'in memory of the spahbed' (بیاد = 'in remembrance of', سپهبد = the military title). گشایم is a literary first‑person verb form (subjunctive/optative) meaning 'I will open' or 'I shall open' (here future sense). دو لب = 'both lips' is the object; opening one's lips is an idiom for 'speaking'.
segments.s209-g71: شوم is an optative/subjunctive first‑person form 'may I be' or 'let me become' (classical usage). هرچ هستند پیوند من is a relative/nominal clause 'whatever my bonds/ties are' (هرچ = 'whatever', هستند = 'are', پیوند من = 'my ties'); word order is inverted for poetic effect, and the clause functions as the complement of شوم: 'may I be whatever my ties are.'
segments.s209-g72: خردمند is 'a wise person'. کو here is the archaic/interrogative particle 'who/which/where is' used to introduce a rhetorical question. بشنود is a subjunctive/potential 'would hear' or 'should hear' and پند من is 'my advice/counsel' as object. Together the clause asks rhetorically 'Which wise person would hear my counsel?'
segments.s209-g81: فراوان here functions as an adverb modifying the verb phrase that follows (“plentifully/many”). فریبش is a bound form meaning “deceit directed at him / his deception” and serves as the direct object of فرستاده‌ام. فرستاده‌ام is the first‑person singular perfect of فرستادن (“I have sent”); the object precedes the verb in this poetic ordering.
segments.s209-g82: ز هر گونه‌ای is a prepositional/partitive phrase “of/from every kind” (ز used poetically as “of/from”), modifying بندها. بندها is the plural noun “fetters/bonds” (here meaning snares or constraints). داده‌ام is the first‑person perfect of دادن (“I have given/placed”) and completes the clause: “I have set/placed fetters of every kind.”
segments.s209-g83: سپاهی ز جنگاوران is a noun phrase with سپاهی used as a collective “a force/contingent” and ز + جنگاوران = “from the warriors.” برگزین is an imperative “choose!” addressing the addressee; the order (object phrase then verb) is typical in elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s209-g84: که introduces a purpose/result clause (“so that / in order that”). بر + زین = “upon the saddle” with بر functioning as the preposition and زین = saddle; شتابش (“his haste”) is the noun carrying the idea of quick action and is the semantic subject of بیاید. بیاید is a subjunctive/jussive third‑person singular (“may come / may mount”) used after که to express purpose or expectation. ز کین (“from/out of vengeance”) gives the motive for the haste.
segments.s209-g85: مگر here means “unless” (introducing the conditional/exceptive clause). بومشان (“their bӧm”) is “their homeland / dwelling,” with the possessive suffix -شان; از marks separation (“from/out of”). بنه is used poetically for the base/roots/foundation (here contextually ‘from its roots’), and برکنیم is the hortative/subjunctive first‑person‑plural “let us remove/uproot.”
segments.s209-g86: بتخت و بگنج are contracted prepositional phrases (poetic spelling of به تخت و به گنج) meaning “to/at the throne and to/into the treasure.” آتش اندر زنیم is an idiomatic verbal phrase: آتش = “fire,” اندر = “in/within,” and زنیم (first‑person‑plural of زدن) is used here idiomatically to mean “set (a fire)/cast fire” or “attack by fire” — so the whole phrase means “we will set fire within the throne and the treasure.” Word order places the affected places (بتخت و بگنج) before the verb for emphasis, typical of epic style.
segments.s209-g87: وگر نه begins a conditional (literally “if not”/“but if not”). ز کین سیاوش is an ablative/motivating phrase “because of Siaush’s vengeance” (ز marking source/reason). سپاه is the subject noun “the army”; in this poetic ordering the conditional preface and motive phrase come before the subject.
segments.s209-g88: نیاساید is a negative stative verb meaning “does not rest / will not be at rest” (here applied in sense of ‘cease from fighting’). از جنگ (“from war”) marks what they do not rest from. هرگز “never” intensifies the negation, and شاه “the king” is coordinated with سپاه by the implied nor/and‑not sense in the line: the verb applies to both “army” and “king.” The inversion and compact negation are characteristic of epic Persian syntax.
segments.s209-g49: کنون is an adverb meaning “now” placed at the clause start. بار تریاک زهر is a noun phrase where بار (“load, burden”) governs the modifiers تریاک (“opium”) and زهر (“poison”): a ‘poisonous load of opium’. آمدست is an archaic perfective/past participial clause-final verb (old Persian form of آمده است) meaning “has come,” so the whole clause states that this poisonous load has arrived.
segments.s209-g50: This clause is an affected-stative construction with the verb آمدست again sentence-final; مرا functions as the dative/accusative ‘to me’ (myself as the affected party) and زو means ‘from it/him’ referring back to the poison. همه رنج ‘all suffering’ is the patient that has come (بهر here has a resultive/for-meaning: ‘for/unto’), so the line says that from that thing all my pain has come to me. Word order is archaic and verb-final; بهر آمدست means ‘has come as/for’ or ‘has resulted.’
segments.s209-g51: The subject phrase دل طوس (the heart of Tūs) precedes the predicate غمگین شد ‘became sad’ (غمگین = sad, شد = became). از کار اوی is an ablative/cause phrase ‘because of his deed’ (از = because of/from, کار = deed/affair, اوی = his – archaic possessive). The overall structure is Subject — Predicate — Cause.
segments.s209-g52: بپیچید is a past-tense verb (third-person) meaning ‘he twisted/writhed/turned’ (the preverb ب- appears with the verb پیجیدن). زان = ‘from that’ (anaphoric to the preceding pain), درد = ‘pain/sorrow’, و links the coordinated noun پیکار (‘battle/struggle’), and اوی is the archaic possessive ‘his’. The clause describes a physical/mental writhing in response to pain and struggle.
segments.s209-g53: چنین ‘thus/so’ qualifies the following action; داد پاسخ is a light verb construction where داد (gave) combines with پاسخ (answer) to mean ‘gave an answer’ (i.e. ‘replied’). که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that…’. از مهر تو is a prepositional phrase ‘from/of your love’ (از = from/of, مهر = love/affection, تو = you).
segments.s209-g54: فراوان is an adverb/adjective ‘abundant/many’; نشانست is an archaic copular/nominal predicate meaning roughly ‘is a sign’ or ‘signs exist’ (نشان + ست). بر چهر تو = ‘on your face’ (بر = on/upon, چهر = face, تو = your). The clause says that many signs of that love appear on your face.
segments.s209-g55: This is a string of imperatives. سر آزاد کن is an order ‘free/release your head’ (سر = head, آزاد = free, کن = do! imperative). دور شو is another imperative pair ‘go away/withdraw’ (دور = away, شو = become/be! imperative). زین میان is an archaic locative/ablative ‘from this midst’ (زین = archaic contraction like از این ‘from this’; میان = middle/between). Together they tell someone to free themselves and move away from the middle of the matter.
segments.s209-g56: ببند is an imperative ‘close!’ (second-person command). این در is the direct object ‘this door/gate’ (این = this, در = door/gate). بیم و راه زیان are coordinated nouns forming the objects of the metaphorical closing: بیم = fear, و = and, راه زیان = ‘the road/path of harm/loss’ (راه = path, زیان = harm/loss). The speaker orders closing the gate to fear and the way of loss.
segments.s209-g89: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. The past verb بشنید (3rd-person past) precedes the subject افراسیاب because of poetic inversion; together they mean 'when Afrasiab heard this speech/these words.' این سخن is the direct object of بشنید and refers to the foregoing message.
segments.s209-g90: سران را بخواند is a past-tense clause in which بخواند (3rd-person past) means 'he summoned/called.' را is the definite/object marker attached to سران ('the chiefs'). از همه انجمن is a prepositional phrase ('from the whole assembly') specifying where the chiefs were summoned from.
segments.s209-g91: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain'/'one' here ('a certain/one army'); لشکری is the object 'an army/troops'; ساخت is the past verb 'he made/raised.' The subject افراسیاب follows the verb (poetic V‑O‑S ordering), yielding 'Afrasiab raised an army.'
segments.s209-g92: که introduces the result/clause linked to the previous action ('so that' / 'and'), تاریک شد is a predicative construction 'became dark' (تاریک adjective + شد auxiliary/past), and چشمه آفتاب ('the spring/source of the sun') is the subject that undergoes that change. The clause therefore expresses the effect: 'so that the sun's spring became dark.'
segments.s209-g93: دهم روز is an adverbial time phrase 'on the tenth day.' لشکر is the subject 'the army.' بپیران is an oblique/dative phrase 'to the elders' (ب‑ marking direction/recipient), and رسید is the past verb 'arrived/reached.' The order places the time first, then subject and indirect object before the verb, which is normal in poetry.
segments.s209-g94: سپاهی is 'a host/army' and is modified by the relative/adverbial کزو ('from which' / 'from whom'), introducing the following result clause. زمین ناپدید شد is the result ('the earth became invisible/vanished'); word order is inverted for poetic effect (one could expect 'زمین ناپدید شد'), so the whole means 'a host so great that the earth became unseen because of it.'
segments.s209-g95: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' لشکر is the subject 'the army.' بیاسود is an older/poetic perfective form related to 'آسودن' meaning 'was at rest/was settled' (poetry often uses such forms). روزی بداد is a compact expression meaning 'a day dawned/appeared' (روزی as 'a day' or 'a day/fortune appearing' and بداد here functions as 'came/was given' in archaic/poetic usage). The clause sets the time/background for what follows.
segments.s209-g96: سپه برگرفت uses برگرفت (past) with سپه 'the host/army' to mean 'the army took up (its arms, standards, or baggage).' و joins the two actions. بنه برنهاد is 'he placed/established the benah' — here بنه is the camp/pavilion/bivouac (a poetic use) and برنهاد is the past verb 'set up/placed on,' so the whole phrase means 'and set up camp.'
segments.s209-g9: This clause is arranged like Persian S‑V‑O with the indirect object fronted: بدو 'to him' (prepositional indirect object), داد 'gave' (past tense, third person singular), and شاه (subject) follows the verb in the poetic word order, with the direct object اختر کاویان 'the Kayanian star' after it. Note that کاویان here qualifies اختر as a genitive/attributive (the star belonging to or associated with the Kayan/Kayanian line).
segments.s209-g10: بران سان (literally 'upon that manner') is an idiomatic poetic phrase meaning 'in that way' or 'so'. که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'which was', with بودی the archaic past form 'was'. به رسم کیان means 'according to the custom of the Kayan (kings)'—به is the adverbial/relational preposition here, رسم 'custom', and کیان names the royal house/kings.
segments.s209-g11: ز اختر 'from the star' is a poetic prepositional phrase placing source first. یکی روز فرخ is a noun phrase 'one/ a certain fortunate (auspicious) day' with فرخ ('fortunate, auspicious') postpositive, and بجست is an archaic past tense verb 'leapt up/ sprang forth' (poetic form).
segments.s209-g12: که here introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'on which' or 'such that'. بیرون شدن is a verbal noun/infinitive-like construction 'the act of going out' (بیرون 'out' + شدن 'to become/go'), and را marks it as the definite/affected element. کی is the interrogative 'when', آید is the present/future sense 'will come/happen', and درست is the predicative adjective 'proper/right'. Together the clause says it was unclear/undetermined when it would be proper to go out.
segments.s209-g13: همی is the archaic/progressive particle used with past tense to mean 'kept on' or 'continued to'. رفت is the past tense verb 'went'. با + کوس خسرو is an accompanying/instrumental phrase 'with Khosrow's drum' (کوس 'war‑drum', خسرو 'Khosrow' as possessive), and بدشت ('to the plain') is a locative phrase marking direction/place.
segments.s209-g14: بدان is the contracted poetic form of به + آن meaning 'to that (place/matter)' (here glossed 'to that [point]'). تا introduces a limit 'until', and سپهبد 'the spahbed' is the subject of the following verb. بدو 'to him/unto him' marks the person affected, and برگذشت is a past tense verb 'passed by/passed over' (بر‑گذشت). The clause reads 'until the spahbed passed by him.'
segments.s209-g15: یکی لشکری is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain army/host' (یکی here 'a certain' rather than strict numeral). همچو introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by the image کوه سیاه 'a black/dark mountain' used to describe the massiveness or darkness of the host.
segments.s209-g16: گذشتند is the past plural verb 'they passed' (poetic agreement with a collective subject). بر پیش is a prepositional complex 'before/in front of', بیدار 'awake/alert' qualifies شاه 'king' (the king is being described as watchful). The whole says the host passed in front of the alert king.
segments.s209-g25: تو is the emphatic/explicit 2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. گفتی is the simple past 2nd‑singular of گفتن ('you said') introducing direct reported speech; what follows is the reported clause. به آب اندر است is a classical locative clause: به ... اندر = 'in/into ...', with اندر an older preposition 'in/within' and است the present copula 'is'. Word order here is somewhat inverted for poetic diction: speaker + گفتی + quoted clause.
segments.s209-g26: سپهر و ستاره is a compound subject 'the sky and the star(s)'. بخواب is a predicative expression meaning 'in sleep / asleep' (here a noun/adverbial sense, not an imperative). اندر است repeats the older locative + copula 'are within / are in', with است used in poetic/classical Persian even after a plural subject.
segments.s209-g27: نهاد is the past tense 'placed / put' (here the verb). از بر is a classical locative phrase ('از' +'بر' = 'on/upon, on the back of'); the direct object مهد ('cradle') appears with the adjective پیروزه ('turquoise') associated with it—poetic word order places modifiers around nouns differently than modern colloquial Persian.
segments.s209-g28: همی رفت is a progressive/continuous past 'kept going' (همی is the classical progressive particle). زین گونه = 'in this manner' (زین = 'this', گونه = 'way/manner'). تا introduces a limit 'until / as far as' and رود شهد is a noun phrase 'the river of honey' (رود = river, شهد = honey); the motion verb رفت governs the whole clause, so 'went on thus until the honey river'.
segments.s209-g29: هیونی 'beast/animal' is the noun subject. بکردار is an adjectival phrase 'of good character / well‑behaved' (ب‑ + کردار). باد here is the optative particle 'may it be' expressing a wish (classical use of باد). دمان is a poetic noun (literally 'hem, skirt') used here in context as a nominal element attached to the clause; its exact nuance is poetic, but grammatically it appears as a substantival element associated with the subject clause.
segments.s209-g30: بدش is بد ('bad') with the enclitic possessive ش = 'its' (i.e. 'its badness'). نزد پیران means 'in the presence of / before the elders' (نزد = near/in front of, پیران = elders). هم 'also / even' adds emphasis, and اندر زمان 'in that time / at that very moment' is a locative/time phrase; together the clause says that its badness remained/was present before the elders even then.
segments.s209-g31: که is the conjunction 'that / for'. من 'I' is the subject. جنگ را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker ('the war'). گردن افراخته is a participial/adjectival phrase 'having lifted the neck' (literally) used idiomatically for taking up war/pridefully going to battle; it modifies the subject (I) or describes the state in which the action occurs.
segments.s209-g32: سوی 'toward(s)' marks direction. رود شهد 'the river (rūd) of honey (shahd)'. آمدم is the past 1st‑singular 'I came'. ساخته is a past participle/adjectival complement 'made / prepared' used here adverbially to mean 'having come prepared' or 'came, being prepared'—classical Persian often places such participial complements after the verb.
segments.s209-g97: This clause uses the poetic preposition 'ز' (from) + noun 'پیمان' to mark what was abandoned, followed by the past verb form 'بگردید' (an archaic/poetic past meaning 'turned away' or 'they turned'); the sequence 'وز یاد عهد' is effectively 'and from the remembrance of the oath' (here 'وز' functions as a fused form of 'و + ز' = 'and from'), so the whole segment lists two things they turned away from.
segments.s209-g98: This clause begins with the verb 'بیامد' (came, past) as the main verb; 'دمان' here is a concrete noun 'skirt/hem' (a poetic image), followed by the limit marker 'تا' = 'as far as' and the locative 'لب رود' = 'the river's brink', with 'شهد' (honey/sweetness) as a descriptive noun attached at the end — overall a pictorial line: the hem came as far as the river's lip, with 'honey' characterizing that place or manner.
segments.s209-g99: 'طلایه' (vanguard) is the subject and is followed by the past verb 'بیامد' (came); 'بنزدیک طوس' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'near Tūs' (بنزدیک = near/close), so the whole unit states that the vanguard approached Tūs.
segments.s209-g100: The relative/causal particle 'که' introduces what follows ('where/that'); 'بربند' here is an archaic/formal verb meaning 'they fastened/tied on' (b- prefix common in classical Persian), 'بر' = 'on/upon', and the phrase 'کوهه پیل' is a poetic compound (literally 'mountain-elephant', i.e. very large elephant) with 'کوس' (war-drum) as the object — together: they fastened drums onto the (huge) elephants.
segments.s209-g101: The particle 'که' introduces a clause; 'پیران' (the elders) is subject and 'نداند' is the negative past/poetic form 'do not/know not' (here present-general sense in narrative), 'سخن' is the object 'speech', and 'جز فریب' ('except deception') limits that object — syntactically: 'that the elders know nothing in speech except deceit.'
segments.s209-g102: 'چو' here = 'when'; 'داند' (they know/realize) introduces the subordinate content clause 'که تنگ اندر آمد' = 'that a tightness came in' (i.e. they were hemmed in / pressed); the final word 'نهیب' is a noun meaning a loud, booming cry — syntactically: 'when they knew that they were pressed tightly, (they) gave a shout.'
segments.s209-g103: 'درفش' (banner/standard) is the subject; 'جفا پیشه' is a compound attributive phrase meaning 'practicing cruelty' or 'cruelty-wielding' (literally 'cruelty-professing'), and the verb phrase 'آمد پدید' (came forth/appeared) is a two-part poetic verb 'آمد' + adjective/adv. 'پدید' meaning 'manifestly appeared' — together: 'the banner of cruelty appeared.'
segments.s209-g104: This clause states the army's deployment: 'سپه' (army) + locative phrase 'بر لب رود' (on the river's brink) followed by the compound verb 'صف برکشید' — 'صف' (battle-line/formation) and 'برکشید' (lifted/raised or drew up) form a classical expression meaning 'drew up their ranks' — hence: 'the army drew up its lines on the river bank.'
segments.s209-g41: وزین روی is an adverbial phrase 'with a dignified/august face' (وزین = 'dignified', روی = 'face/appearance') describing manner. لشکر is the beneficiary/indirect object ('to/for the army') with the preposition omitted (typical in poetic Persian). بیاورد is the past 3rd-person singular 'he brought'; طوس is the postverbal subject (proper name) — classical Persian often places the subject after the verb.
segments.s209-g42: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing what was brought: درفش همایون ('the auspicious/imperial standard'), و پیلان ('and elephants') و کوس ('and the war-drum'). همایون here is an epithet applied to the banner ('imperial/august'); و is the simple conjunction linking items.
segments.s209-g43: سپهدار پیران is a titled name phrase: 'the commander Pīrān' (here پیران functions as the proper name Pīrān). یکی is the indefinite 'a certain/one', and چرپ گوی is a compound: چرپ 'crooked/deceitful' modifying گوی (agent noun 'speaker' / 'one who speaks'), so 'a crooked‑tongued man' or 'a deceitful speaker.'
segments.s209-g44: ز ترکان is an ablative/partitive 'from (among) the Turks' (ز = 'from'), and فرستاد is the past 3rd-person 'sent'. نزدیک اوی is a prepositional/locative phrase 'near him' where اوی is the archaic third‑person pronoun 'him/it'. The noun phrase 'یکی چرپ گوی ز ترکان' is the direct object placed before the verb (common in poetry).
segments.s209-g45: بگفت is the past narrative 'he said'. آنک introduces the content clause ('that/which'), so the following clause gives what was said. من با فرنگیس و شاه is a topicalized phrase 'I, with Farangis and the king' (با marks accompaniment), with من placed for emphasis.
segments.s209-g46: چه کردم is a fused interrogative/relative 'what I did' (چه = 'what', کردم = 'I did' past). ز خوبی uses ز (classical form of از) to indicate cause/reason 'from/because of goodness/kindness'. بهر جایگاه is a purpose/beneficiary phrase 'for the sake of a position/post' (بهر = 'for', جایگاه = 'position/status'). The whole phrase gives the content of what was said: my actions motivated by kindness for a post.
segments.s209-g47: ز indicates the source/cause 'from/because of' (ز درد سیاوش = 'from Siavash's pain/sorrow'). درد here means emotional sorrow, and خروشان modifies بدم: this is a nominal phrase where خروشان ('roaring, surging') describes بدم ('my blood') — a poetic, elliptical way to say 'my blood surged/roared' (بدم here functions as 'my blood').
segments.s209-g48: چو introduces a simile 'as/like'. بر آتش تیز جوشان is a prepositional phrase 'upon/on the sharp, seething fire' with two attributive adjectives (تیز = 'sharp', جوشان = 'boiling/seething'). The final بدم again is 'my blood' (elliptically completing the simile: 'my blood surged as upon the sharp, seething fire').
segments.s209-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'he heard' placed before its subject (typical Classical Persian word order). پیران is the subject (the proper name Piran). غمی گشت سخت is a result clause — گشت 'became' with غمی 'sorrow/grief' as the state he entered, and سخت intensifies it ('severely/very').
segments.s209-g34: فروبست is a past verb meaning 'closed/packed up' (he closed up/packed up). بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. پیل here is 'elephant'. ناکام is an adjective 'defeated/unsuccessful' modifying پیل, and رخت is the noun 'baggage/camp gear' — together giving the image 'he packed up his baggage on the defeated elephant'.
segments.s209-g35: برون‌رفت here functions as a past action 'he went out / sallied forth' (verb phrase). با introduces the comitative phrase 'with'. نامداران is 'the renowned/illustrious ones'. خوی + ش together form the possessive 'his own' (خوی 'kin/own', ش the clitic 'his').
segments.s209-g36: گزیده is an adjective 'chosen/selected' modifying the following nouns. دلاور is 'valiant/brave', and سواران is 'horsemen/riders'; خوی + ش again mark possession 'his'. The phrase is a noun group 'his chosen valiant horsemen' with modifiers preceding the head noun (typical poetic order).
segments.s209-g37: The initial که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'. ایران سپه را is 'Iran's army' with را marking the definite object/topic. ببیند is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form meaning 'he may see' (purpose). The second که introduces the embedded interrogative 'what ... is' (کِی/چیست follows). چی is the question word 'what' and ست is the copula 'is'.
segments.s209-g38: سرافراز is an adjective 'honored/triumphant'. چندست is a fused interrogative 'how many / how many are there' (here asking the number/extent). و is the conjunction 'and'. با introduces the prepositional phrase 'with'. طوس is the proper name Tous (the hero). کیست is the interrogative copula 'who is (with him)?' — together: 'how many are triumphant, and who is with Tous.'
segments.s209-g39: رده برکشیدند is a past, 3rd-person plural verb phrase 'they drew up/raised their ranks' (رده 'rank/line', برکشیدند 'they raised/drew up'). زان سوی رود is a locative phrase 'on that side of the river' — زان 'from/that', سوی 'side/toward', رود 'river'.
segments.s209-g40: فرستاد is a past 3rd-person singular verb 'he sent'. نزد marks the recipient 'to/near' in the sense 'to the presence of'. سپهبد is the addressee title 'spahbed' (a senior commander). درود is the noun 'greeting' — together 'he sent a greeting to the spahbed.'
segments.s209-g153: غمی (a noun with the -ی suffix) is the theme 'a sorrow' and گشت is the past-tense verb 'became/ befell' (گشت = ‚it became/it came to be‘). پیران ('the elders') follows the verb by poetic inversion and is the affected group. ز + توران سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from/ because of the Turanian host' that gives the cause.
segments.s209-g154: ز ترکان is a prepositional phrase 'of/from the Turks'; تهی ماند is a compound predicate 'remained empty' (تهی 'empty/devoid' + ماند 'remained/was left'). آوردگاه ('battlefield') appears at the end by inversion and is the locus that 'remained empty of the Turks.'
segments.s209-g155: دلیران توران is a noun phrase 'the brave ones of Turan' (دلیران plural adjective/noun modified by توران). و connects a second group, کنداوران; that final word here names the second group (treated as a plural noun), appearing in apposition with دلیران.
segments.s209-g156: کشیدند is the past-tense 3rd-person-plural verb 'they drew' (here 'drew' a weapon). Its direct objects follow: شمشیر 'sword' and گرز گران 'heavy mace(s)' (گران qualifying گرز). The verb-before-object order is normal in narrative past tense.
segments.s209-g157: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of their resolve. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether'. بکوشیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortatory 'let us strive' (ب- prefix + subjunctive ending -یم). و links the next clause; جنگ آوریم is a verbal phrase where آوریم ('we bring/ cause') takes جنگ as its object: literal 'we bring war' = 'we wage war.'
segments.s209-g158: جهان is the (figurative) subject 'the world / this life'; بر دل طوس is a prepositional phrase 'upon Tūs's heart' (بر = 'upon', دل = 'heart', طوس = the hero Tūs). تنگ آوریم is a causative idiomatic turn: آوردن + تنگ 'to bring narrowness' = 'to make [someone] distressed/afflicted'. The verb آوریم is again 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortatory 'let us cause.'
segments.s209-g159: چنین 'thus/so' introduces reported speech. گفت here is the verb 'said' (past), followed by the speaker's name هومان. که introduces the content of what he said. امروز 'today' and جنگ 'battle/war' begin the reported command.
segments.s209-g160: بسازید is 2nd-person-plural imperative/subjunctive 'make/wage' (here 'make war'); و links the second command. دل را uses را as the definite object marker ('the heart' as object). مدارید is a present/subjunctive/imperative-form verb from مدارا کردن 'to treat/be lenient', used here in a causative-style instruction with the following predicate تنگ: دل را مدارید تنگ = 'make the heart constricted' (i.e. cause distress). Word order places the object before the verb and its complement, typical in Persian imperative constructions.
segments.s209-g1: درم داد is a short past clause: درم (dirham) is the direct object placed before the verb داد (gave), so literally 'gave a dirham'. و links this clause to the next. روزی‌دهان را is a definite direct object (را marks it) and بخواند is Ferdowsi's past form of خواندن here meaning 'call/summon' (3rd pers. sing.), so روزی‌دهان را بخواند = 'he summoned the provider.'
segments.s209-g2: بسی is an adverb (here 'much' / 'a great deal'); با + سپهبد is a prepositional phrase 'with the spahbed'. سخنها is the plural noun 'words/speeches' (no را so presented as topic), and براند is past 3sg of راندن 'to drive/propel' used idiomatically to mean 'utter/let loose' (i.e. 'uttered/exchanged many words'). Word order: adverb + prepositional phrase + object + verb.
segments.s209-g3: همان is a determiner 'that (very/aforementioned)'; رای زد is a light idiom where رای (opinion/plan) + زد (past of زدن used here as 'put forward / give') = 'he voiced/gave that counsel/plan'. با تهمتن is 'with Tahmatan' (an epithet/name in the text). بر آن is a prepositional phrase 'on/about that' (بر indicating 'on, concerning').
segments.s209-g4: چنین 'thus/so' introduces the manner. تا 'until' governs the following clause. رخ روز is a nominal compound 'the face of day' (i.e. dawn); شد is the past of شدن 'became/appeared'. در نهان is a prepositional adverbial 'in secret/secretly' modifying the action—so together: 'thus until, in secret, the face of day appeared.'
segments.s209-g5: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. بر زد سنان is a poetic word order where بر + زد (past of زدن 'to strike') + سنان (spear) = 'struck upon the spear' (i.e. 'the sun struck the spear'); از نشیب 'from the descent/slope' is an origin/locative phrase attached to the action.
segments.s209-g6: شتاب آمد is an idiomatic nominal+verb expression meaning 'came with haste' or 'hurried'. از رفتن is a prepositional/gerundial phrase 'from [the act of] going' (here functioning adverbially to indicate departure), and با نهیب 'with a shout/with a thunderous cry' further modifies the motion. Overall: 'he hurried away with a cry.'
segments.s209-g7: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (a high military commander). بیامد is past 'came'. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/near the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه = 'to the king's presence' or 'near the king').
segments.s209-g8: This segment lists accompanying names and forces. گیو and گودرز are proper names; و چندی سپاه means 'and some troops' (چندی = 'several/some', سپاه = 'army/troops'). ابا in this line is ambiguous: it does not match the supplied senses ('to refuse' / 'reluctance') and here functions like a vocative/name or an introductory item in the list (so I mark its sense as unknown).
segments.s209-g73: This is a noun phrase + verb line with poetic word order: بایران ('to Iran') is the beneficiary/goal of the verb گذارم ('I leave/put'); بر و بوم و رخت is a coordinated list of direct objects meaning 'land, home and belongings'. گذارم is 1st-person present/future-like (classical diction for 'I will leave/entrust').
segments.s209-g74: سر نامور is a noun phrase with سر ('head, chief') modified by نامور ('renowned'); بهتر از introduces a comparison 'better than' where از functions as the comparative marker, followed by the two items joined by و (تاج و تخت 'crown and throne').
segments.s209-g75: وزین گفتتها is an adjective (وزین 'weighty, grave') modifying گفتتها ('utterances/sayings'); the clause uses بود (past 'was') + predicate تهی ('empty') with مغزش ('his brain') as the subject/possessive subject — literally 'of those weighty sayings his brain was empty', i.e. he lacked understanding.
segments.s209-g76: همی is a customary classical adverb meaning 'continually/ever'; جست here is a past iterative verb 'sought/kept seeking'; نو روزگار بهی is the object phrase 'a new good/fortunate time' where نو modifies روزگار and بهی qualifies it as 'good/excellent'.
segments.s209-g77: This is a verbal clause where هیونی (a wild/monstrous creature) is the (implied) object and برافگند is a past tense verb 'threw off/let loose'; هنگام خواب is a temporal adverbial 'at the time of sleep' modifying the action.
segments.s209-g78: فرستاد is the past tense verb 'sent/dispatched' with an implied object (the creature just mentioned); نزدیک is an adverb/prepositional adverb 'near/toward' and افراسیاب is the place/person toward whom it was sent.
segments.s209-g79: کزایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' that governs the motion verb آمد ('came'); سپاه is the subject 'army/host'. The following و پیل و کوس list accompaniments ('and elephants and drums') appended to the announcement of the army.
segments.s209-g80: همان here is a demonstrative 'that/namely' introducing a list of proper names; the names are simply coordinated with و ('and') in a standard enumerative sequence, ending with طوس (here the hero Tous rather than the city).
segments.s209-g129: بناکام می‌رفت is an imperfect/progressive narrative: می‌رفت (was going) with the adjective بِنَکام (unsuccessful) describing the subject's departing state. باید ز دهر is an impersonal, idiomatic comment: باید here functions like “it must be / it is due to” and ز دهر (‘from Time’ / ‘from Fate’) gives the cause — “it is by Fate.”
segments.s209-g130: چه ... چه ... sets up a correlative ‘whether ... or ...’ construction. زو is the archaic clitic form of از او (‘for/for him’ or ‘concerning him’), بهر marks purpose/benefit (‘for’), تریاک is literal ‘opium/opiate’, and یابی is a present/subjunctive-type form (‘one may find / you may obtain’). The final چه introduces the second alternative (زهر = ‘poison’).
segments.s209-g131: ندانم is the negative present of دانستن (‘I do not know’). سرانجام و فرجام are synonymous nouns placed in coordination (both mean ‘outcome/end’). The clause ends with چیست (چی + ست), the copula-question form, literally ‘what is (the outcome)?’ — standard Classical/poetic word order places the copula at the end.
segments.s209-g132: برین is the older/poetic fusion of بر + این (‘on/over this’), so برین رفتن = ‘this going/departure’ (a nominalized phrase). اکنون is an adverb ‘now’; بباید is impersonal ‘one must/it is necessary’; گریست is an infinitival/nominal verb here meaning ‘to weep/one should weep.’ Together: ‘As for this departure, now one must weep.’
segments.s209-g133: یکی introduces an indefinite (‘a certain’). نامداری is an attributive noun meaning ‘renown/renowned.’ The phrase بد ارژنگ نام follows the naming formula X نام (‘named X’); here بد ارژنگ functions as the personal name (i.e. “Bad‑Arzhang”), so نام at the end identifies that as his name. (بد here appears as part of the proper name rather than its literal meaning.)
segments.s209-g134: بابر is the personal name (subject). اندر آورده is a past/perfect-like compound (اندر + آورده) meaning ‘has brought’ or idiomatically ‘has gained/earned.’ از جنگ نام is the idiom ‘a name from battle’ = ‘fame won in war’ (literally ‘a name from the fighting’). So: ‘Babr gained fame in the battle.’
segments.s209-g135: برآورد is a past narrative verb (compound of بر + آورد) here meaning ‘drew forth / sent out’ (an archaic poetic phrasing). از دشت specifies origin ‘from the plain’; آورد is another past form ‘brought’; گرد is used in a spatial/collective sense (archaic) indicating the host/roundness or a gathered body — overall: ‘(he) drew forth out of the plain and brought (his) host.’
segments.s209-g136: از ایرانیان جست uses جست (past of جستن) in the sense ‘he leapt/sprung (upon)’ — a common epic usage for sudden attack. چندی is temporal ‘for a while’ and نبرد is the nominal ‘battle/fighting’ (so چندی نبرد = ‘he fought for some time’ or ‘he engaged in several bouts of battle’).
segments.s209-g161: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an adverbial/particle meaning 'now/just now' (poetic); زیشان is a fused preposition + pronoun (ز + ایشان) meaning 'of them / among them'. یکی نامور literally 'one renowned' uses یکی as 'a certain / one (person)' placed before the adjective, so the phrase means 'a certain famed man among them.'
segments.s209-g162: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from / of the host' (ز + لشکر). برارد here is historically ambiguous in the manuscript: syntactically it functions as the verbal idea 'raise' (برآرد) — 'should lift' — but the supplied lemma senses appear as a noun; since the context requires a verbal reading treat it as the 3rd‑person action 'raise/lift'. به پیکار is 'to battle', and سر is the literal 'head' used in the idiom 'سر به پیکار برآرد' = 'lift his head to fight.'
segments.s209-g163: فرستیم is first‑person plural hortative/optative 'let us send' or 'we will send'. گردی دمان is an adverbial/nominal phrase: گردی here (literally 'wandering' / 'a round') means 'a scouting round' or 'a detachment to circle', and دمان 'the skirt/edge' (of the plain or hill); together 'send a round to the skirt.' The initial word پذیره in this context names what is sent (an agent or party) but the listed senses ('reception/shelter') don't match a scouting sense, so it is likely a context‑specific term here.
segments.s209-g164: ببینیم is hortative/subjunctive first plural 'let us see'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that') and governs the subjunctive گردد 'may turn / will come about'. بر که گردد زمان is literally 'upon whom the time turns' — که is a relative/interrogative 'who/whom', بر = 'toward / upon', and گردد is the verb 'turn / come to be'.
segments.s209-g165: نجویید is a negative hortative/imperative 'do not seek / do not attempt' (ن + جویید). بتندی is an adverb/verb form from تندیدن meaning 'to be swift / hasten' and here modifies the action (i.e. 'in haste' or 'by hurrying'). وزیشان appears to be a fused particle (و ز ایشان 'and/of them') or a scribal contraction; the supplied lexical senses ('gust / weight') do not match the syntactic role here, so treat it as a connective 'and / from them'. جنگ is the object 'war / battle.'
segments.s209-g166: بباید is an impersonal modal 'it is necessary / one must'. امروز is the temporal adverb 'today'. کردن is the light verb used with the noun درنگ to form the verbal phrase درنگ کردن 'to delay'; here کردن functions as auxiliary ('to do / to make'). درنگ is the noun 'delay / tarrying' and is the complement of کردن. The whole clause expresses obligation not to delay today.
segments.s209-g167: بدانگه is a temporal adverb 'thereupon / at that moment'. که introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'when' ('that/when the army...'). لشکر is the subject 'the army/host' and بجنبد is the present/subjunctive 'stirs / moves' (3rd person). ز جای is the prepositional phrase 'from its place' (ز = from, جای = place).
segments.s209-g168: برآید is a 3rd‑person verb 'arises / appears'. تبیره is the grammatical subject here; the literal listed senses are 'interpretation / explanation', and in context it functions as 'an announcement / utterance / explanation' that issues forth. ز پرده‌سرای is a locative/source phrase 'from the curtain‑house / pavilion / stage' (ز = from, پرده‌سرای = curtain‑hall/stage), so the clause says that this utterance appears from behind the curtain/pavilion.
